statement.markdown: Given an array of integers `heights` representing the histogram's bar heights where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer n, the number of bars in the histogram.
The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the heights of the bars.
statement.outputFormat: The area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

constraints.description: The number of bars in the histogram.
The height of each bar in the histogram.

examples.explanation:
The above is a histogram where width of each bar is 1.
The largest rectangle is the one with height 5 and width 2, which has an area of 10 units.
The largest rectangle has an area of 2 units.

hints.text: Check if there is at least one bar in the histogram.
Initialize variables for maximum area and current area.
Iterate through the histogram, updating maximum area and current area.
Check if the current bar is wider than the current area. If so, update maximum area with current area.
If the current bar is not wider, update current area with the height of the current bar.

editorial.title: Optimal Approach to Largest Rectangle in Histogram
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that a larger rectangle can be formed by extending a smaller bar to its left and right boundaries. A stack data structure can be used to efficiently track these boundaries.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve iterating over all pairs of bars and calculating the maximum area that can be formed with each pair as the smallest bar. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(n^2), making it slow for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a stack to keep track of indices of increasing bars allows us to efficiently find the left and right boundaries for each bar, reducing the time complexity to O(n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a stack to store indices of the bars. It iterates over the histogram, popping the stack and calculating the area for each bar when a smaller bar is encountered or when the end of the histogram is reached.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that each bar is pushed and popped from the stack exactly once, and that the area calculations are accurate. The stack ensures that the left boundary for each bar is the first bar to its left that is smaller, and the right boundary is the first bar to its right that is smaller.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with a sample input, such as [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3], demonstrates that the optimal solution correctly calculates the maximum area as 10.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly calculating the width of the rectangle
Failing to handle the case when the stack is empty
Not considering the end of the histogram when popping the stack
Using a queue instead of a stack
Not updating the maximum area correctly
Not handling the case when two bars have the same height

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
heights = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
stack = []
max_area = 0
for i in range(n + 1):
    while stack and (i == n or heights[stack[-1]] > heights[i]):
        h = heights[stack.pop()]
        w = i if not stack else i - stack[-1] - 1
        max_area = max(max_area, h * w)
    stack.append(i)
print(max_area)
